answer:
I have everything I need. Here's the contract your custom router must satisfy.

## The `Router<T>` Contract

The contract is defined in [`src/router.ts`](src/router.ts) as a tiny three-member interface:

```ts
export interface Router<T> {
  name: string
  add(method: string, path: string, handler: T): void
  match(method: string, path: string): Result<T>
}
```

### Member-by-member

**1. `name: string`** — A human-readable identifier (e.g. `'RegExpRouter'`). Note that `SmartRouter` mutates its own `name` after selecting a child router (to something like `'SmartRouter + RegExpRouter'`), so any introspection code must tolerate that — but your own router just needs to set a stable string.

**2. `add(method, path, handler)`** — Register a route. `method` is a lowercase HTTP verb or the sentinel `METHOD_NAME_ALL` (`'ALL'`) for all methods. `path` uses Hono's path grammar (`:param`, `*`, `:param{regex}`, etc.). If you don't support a given path shape, **throw `UnsupportedPathError`** (also exported from `src/router.ts`) — that's the signal `SmartRouter` catches to fall back to another router.

**3. `match(method, path): Result<T>`** — Return matched handlers. `Result<T>` has **two valid shapes** and consumers downstream handle both:

```ts
export type Result<T> =
  | [[T, ParamIndexMap][], ParamStash]   // shape A: param indices + shared stash
  | [[T, Params][]]                      // shape B: resolved param map per handler
```

- **Shape A** (what `RegExpRouter` uses): handlers carry a `ParamIndexMap` (`{ name: index }`) and a shared `ParamStash` (`string[]`) holds the captured values. Efficient when params are extracted via a single regex execution.
- **Shape B** (what `TrieRouter`/`PatternRouter` use): each handler is paired with a fully-resolved `Params` (`{ name: value }`) map. Simpler, no shared stash.

Pick whichever is natural for your implementation — both are fully supported.

### Hard constraints from ADR-02

These are architectural invariants the codebase depends on (sourced from the intent registry on `Router` and `RegExpRouter`):

1. **Full interface, no partials.** Preset swappability and downstream `compose()`/context construction assume all three members exist and behave as specified.
2. **Support the full path grammar** accepted by any existing router — otherwise swapping your router in would silently drop routes that used to work. Use the shared conformance suite at [`src/router/common.case.test.ts`](src/router/common.case.test.ts) (the `runTest({ newRouter })` helper) — every built-in router runs against it.
3. **Throw `UnsupportedPathError`** (not a generic `Error`) for path shapes you can't encode, if you want to be usable inside `SmartRouter`. Also, throwing after the matcher is "built" (if applicable) conventionally uses the `MESSAGE_MATCHER_IS_ALREADY_BUILT` message.
4. **Result shape must be one of the two above** and stable per-call — consumers in `hono-base.ts`, `compose()`, and `app.routes` introspection branch on it.
5. **Benchmarks.** If you're upstreaming, new routers are expected to come with benchmark coverage alongside the existing ones in `benchmarks/`.

### How to plug it in

You don't need a preset — users can pass any `Router<T>` directly:

```ts
new Hono({ router: new MyRouter() })
```

That's the public seam. Presets (`hono`, `hono/quick`, `hono/tiny`) just pre-wire `SmartRouter` with specific children, but the `router:` option in `HonoOptions` accepts anything implementing `Router<T>`.

### Recommended scaffolding

1. Implement the interface against [`src/router.ts`](src/router.ts).
2. Import and run [`src/router/common.case.test.ts`](src/router/common.case.test.ts)'s `runTest` with `newRouter: () => new MyRouter()` — this is the de-facto conformance test. You can pass a `skip` list for path shapes you deliberately don't support (but document why; `RegExpRouter` itself does this for dynamic patterns it can't encode).
3. Look at `RegExpRouter` (shape A), `TrieRouter`, and `PatternRouter` (shape B) as three distinct reference implementations of the same contract.

SYM RegExpRouter@src/router/reg-exp-router/router.ts:122 class
  SIG class RegExpRouter<T> implements Router<T>
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "All router implementations must implement the full Router<T> interface"
    RATIONALE "Partial implementations would break preset swappability and the shared contract that everything downstream depends on"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "Result<T> has a dual shape ([[T, ParamIndexMap][], ParamStash] | [[T, Params][]]); consumers of router output must handle both representations"
    RATIONALE "The dual shape is part of the public contract; consumers assuming one form will break when active router uses the other"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "SmartRouter mutates its own name on first match (e.g., 'SmartRouter' → 'SmartRouter + RegExpRouter'); introspection code reading router.name must tolerate this transition"
    RATIONALE "Tests and dev tools reading router.name will see the name change after first match"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "Presets are separate module graphs (different entry points), not runtime configuration flags"
    RATIONALE "Bundler must be able to ship different code per preset so unused router builder code is not included"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "The Router<T> interface is part of the public API; breaking changes to it are breaking changes to user code"
    RATIONALE "Users implement Router<T> for custom routers, so the interface is their API, not just internal abstraction"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "Removing presets like hono/quick or hono/tiny is a breaking change; adding new presets is additive and safe"
    RATIONALE "Preset entry points are a public API commitment to downstream users"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "Runtime behavior may differ between presets for the same app; documentation must flag which path shapes are universally supported across all presets"
    RATIONALE "PatternRouter uses platform URLPattern with different semantics than RegExpRouter+TrieRouter"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "Benchmarks must cover each router; removing routers or presets without benchmark evidence is not supported"
    RATIONALE "Multi-router strategy requires comparative data to keep preset rationale valid"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "Consumers of router output (compose(), context construction, app.routes introspection) must handle both Result<T> representations"
    RATIONALE "The active router determines which representation is returned"
  INTENT ADR-02 hard "New routers must support the full path grammar accepted by any existing router"
    RATIONALE "Otherwise a preset swap would silently change which routes work — a breakage no test can reliably catch"
  INTENT ADR-02 soft "Users needing fully deterministic router behavior should pass a concrete router instead of a SmartRouter-based preset"
    RATIONALE "SmartRouter's self-mutation on first match makes behavior non-deterministic until selection"
  INTENT ADR-02 context "Router interface is defined as { name, add(method, path, handler), match(method, path): Result<T> }"
    RATIONALE "Defines the shared contract all routers adhere to"
  INTENT ADR-02 context "SmartRouter tries routers in order on first match, catches UnsupportedPathError, locks to the first succeeding router, and rebinds its match method to the winner"
    RATIONALE "Pays router selection cost once rather than per-request"
  INTENT ADR-02 context "Three presets ship as separate entry points: hono (SmartRouter[RegExpRouter, TrieRouter]), hono/quick (SmartRouter[LinearRouter, TrieRouter]), hono/tiny (PatternRouter alone)"
    RATIONALE "Each preset targets a different performance shape (long-running, edge cold-start, minimum-bundle)"
  INTENT ADR-02 context "Users may bypass presets by passing any Router<T> directly via new Hono({ router: ... })"
    RATIONALE "The Router interface is a public API for user-supplied implementations"
  INTENT ADR-02 context "RegExpRouter throws UnsupportedPathError on path shapes it can't encode"
    RATIONALE "This is what SmartRouter catches to fall back to the next router"

    24	/**

    34

    41	   */

    43	
    44	  /**
    45	   * Matches a route based on the given method and path.

    58	/**

    62	/**
    63	 * Type representing a map of parameters.

    66	/**

    69	 * The result can be in one of two formats:

    72	 *
    73	 * Example:
    74	 *
    75	 * [[handler, paramIndexMap][], paramArray]
    76	 * ```typescript

    78	 *   [

    80	 *     [funcA,       {'id': 0}],              // '/user/:id/*'
    81	 *     [funcB,       {'id': 0, 'action': 1}], // '/user/:id/:action'

    86	 *

    94	 *   ]
    95	 * ]
    96	 * ```
    97	 */
    98	export type Result<T> = [[T, ParamIndexMap][], ParamStash] | [[T, Params][]]
